name: APEM HF55 (HF5844)
statusNote: Active joystick base in ThumbJoy (custom PN HF5844, 500kbps CAN firmware). Known issues: Z-axis spring too soft (1.3N), NOT actually waterproof despite IP67 claim (confirmed by APEM). Merten emailed APEM (Mar 31) requesting stiffer springs + alternatives. Z-AXIS TEST: McMaster 5108N98 spring tested for Z-axis stiffness (week of 3/24), currently installed in Blue Prototype at shop. X/Y AXIS TEST: 7 Lee Spring variants ordered (3/30, Order #260139265) to test different stiffnesses — mechanical testing before custom production.
ipRating: IP67 (CLAIMED — not actually waterproof per APEM)
pros: Compact fingertip form factor
Hall effect — no contact wear
Hollow shaft for wire routing
Custom 500kbps CAN available (HF5844)
Already integrated into ThumbJoy system
Proven in-field (prototype testing)
cons: Spring WAY too soft — 1.3N breakout gives no feedback at speed
NOT actually waterproof despite IP67 label (confirmed by APEM)
Shaft too tight for 6 Melexis wires
Internal epoxy seal cracks easily
Custom firmware may have long lead times
suitabilityReason: Proven platform but spring force and waterproofing failures are deal-breakers for marine hydrofoil use. Good enough for prototyping, not for production.

name: JR Merritt CS3 Series
statusNote: REJECTED: IP65 only — does NOT meet IP67 requirement for marine use (confirmed 2026-03-31)

videos.title: JR Merritt CS3 Joystick at ICUEE (Official)

axes: Multi-axis + optional 3rd axis (twist handle OR thumbwheel)
pros: Explicitly designed for marine/boat applications
Twist handle AND thumbwheel options — both available
Configurable spring forces — can get stiff feel
CAN bus output option
Hall effect paddle-style thumbwheel sold separately
US manufacturer — easier communication, faster shipping
Industrial-grade build quality
cons: Hollow shaft availability unknown — CRITICAL to verify
Industrial/heavy appearance — may need grip customization
Lead times for custom configs unknown
Price likely higher than APEM
suitabilityReason: REJECTED — IP65 only, does not meet mandatory IP67 waterproofing requirement. Per Merten (2026-03-31).

name: JR Merritt VCS2
statusNote: REJECTED: No confirmed IP67 rating — fails mandatory waterproofing requirement (2026-03-31)
pros: Smaller footprint than CS3 — may fit ThumbJoy housing better
Same configurability as CS3
Multi-axis with twist/thumbwheel option
cons: Less force feedback than full-size CS3
Hollow shaft unconfirmed
Less marine-specific documentation
suitabilityReason: REJECTED — No confirmed IP67 rating. Fails mandatory waterproofing requirement. Per Merten (2026-03-31).

name: Elobau J4F / J5 Series
statusNote: Tuesday deep-dive complete (2026-04-07). J4F is robust and truly IP67 on electronics but physically large/heavy for ThumbJoy. Verified dimensions, connector, CAN details, and load specs. Still no confirmed hollow shaft path for internal Melexis wiring.
axes: 2-axis base (X/Y ±20°). J4 platform supports Z-load and variants, but J4F twist implementation remains unconfirmed for this exact config.
output: J1939 CAN (J4F safety config, 250 kbps), 9–32V. J4 base family also supports analog/redundant/CANopen variants.
breakoutForce: Not published in available J4F docs; heavy-duty class. (J4 base supports detent/friction/interlock modes.)
operatingForce: Not explicitly published for J4F in reviewed docs; static load class is very high (2000N X/Y, 700N Z at 190mm lever).
twistTorque: Not published for J4F config. Z-axis load capacity listed for J4 base family (700N at 190mm) but no confirmed spring-return twist torque curve.
ipRating: IP67 electronics (also listed as IP6K7K for J4F electronics); mechanics/thumbwheel on J4F comparison listed IP54
hollowShaft: Unknown; likely non-hollow/limited routing (zinc die-cast shaft + base Deutsch connector architecture)
lifecycle: 2 million cycles (J4F base), handle button/thumbwheel in J4F comparison: 2 million
mounting: Top-mount, 87mm under-panel depth, 106x106mm base, 87.7x87.7mm hole pattern, Deutsch DT04-6P connector
pros: Real marine/off-highway robustness with IP67 electronics
CAN J1939 safety module support, 9–32V system-friendly
Very high static load capacity (X/Y 2000N, Z 700N @190mm)
Detent/friction/center-interlock actuation modes on J4 base family
Modular handle ecosystem with button and thumbwheel options
Known connector and mounting geometry (Deutsch DT04-6P, top-mount)
cons: Too large/heavy for palm-sized ThumbJoy target (334.6mm overall, ~1.26kg base+handle class)
No verified hollow shaft/internal wire path for custom Melexis routing
No published breakout/operating force curves for J4F in retrieved docs
Twist (Z-axis spring-return) not confirmed for this exact J4F configuration
J4F safety CAN profile appears fixed to J1939 250 kbps unless custom arrangement
suitabilityReason: Strong industrial benchmark and genuine environmental sealing, but likely too big for ThumbJoy and still unclear on internal wire routing + Z-twist behavior. Best treated as a robust architecture reference unless Kai accepts a larger controller envelope.
dimensions: Joystick system (J4 + 36xG): total height 334.6mm, above panel 247.6mm, below panel 87mm, base 106x106mm, weight ~1260g

requirementsCheck.xy_with_y_gate: PARTIAL (detent/interlock options; explicit Y-only gate not yet confirmed)

name: OTTO Controls JHM Series
statusNote: MIL-spec heritage, dual magnet reliability
pros: Military/marine heritage — built for harsh environments
Dual magnet design for reliability
Shallow behind-panel depth — good for compact housing
US manufacturer
Full-function medium size
cons: No standard Z-axis twist — would need modification or separate control
Hollow shaft uncertain
Military pricing tends to be premium
Less configurability than JR Merritt
suitabilityReason: Rock-solid reliability but may lack Z-axis twist. Good backup option if custom twist mechanism is added externally.

name: Curtiss-Wright / Penny+Giles JC6000
statusNote: Very stiff feel — designed for 7-20 tonne vehicles
pros: Extremely stiff feel — strong force feedback
IP66 sealing
Hall effect or pot options
CAN bus output
Range of ergonomic handle designs
Custom spring-force adjustments available
Proven in off-highway and marine
cons: May be TOO stiff / heavy for fingertip operation
Likely too large for ThumbJoy beer-can housing
No twist axis
Hollow shaft very unlikely
Heavy-duty = heavy weight
suitabilityReason: Great reference for spring force but likely too bulky and lacks twist. JC3000 fingertip version might be more relevant.

name: RunnTech RT600
statusNote: Cheap, compact, 3-axis with twist — great for testing
pros: Very compact — 36x36mm mounting
Native 3-axis with twist knob
Hall effect throughout
Self-centering spring return
Extremely affordable for prototyping
Multiple grip options (pushbutton, bare knob)
Boat/vessel applications listed by manufacturer
cons: No IP rating — not waterproof
Chinese quality — durability unknown
Analog output only (no CAN) — would feed into Teensy ADC
Spring force likely soft (fingertip class)
No marine track record
Hollow shaft unlikely at this size
suitabilityReason: Best low-cost prototyping option. Test the 3-axis twist concept quickly before committing to an expensive industrial joystick. Not production-worthy.

name: Ruffy Controls HE3 Series
statusNote: Sample purchased 2026-03-30 for hands-on evaluation. True IP67, redundant Hall sensors, 3-axis (Z-twist), 12-bit, configurable — designed for marine/industrial.
axes: 2-axis standard, 3-axis with Z-twist option (40° X/Y, 90° Z)
sensorType: Hall Effect — REDUNDANT (dual sensor per axis, safety-critical applications)
output: Analog (0–5V ratiometric), multiple output options configurable
breakoutForce: Max applied force: 150 lbF (667N) — extremely robust
operatingForce: Spring return, stiff feel — heavy duty
twistRotation: 90° total Z-axis (45° each direction), spring return
hollowShaft: Unknown — needs confirmation. 12 wires per pinout suggests wiring exits at base, not through shaft.
price: $210.60 (2-axis base), price increases for 3-axis and redundant options
pros: TRUE IP67 — 'minimum' IP67 means exceeds the standard
REDUNDANT Hall sensors — dual sensor per axis for safety-critical use (marine, military, industrial)
3-axis with Z-twist available (same as ThumbJoy need)
12-bit resolution — high precision
5V supply — compatible with existing ThumbJoy architecture
Extreme robustness: 150 lbF force rating, 5M cycles
Military/SAE vibration tested (MIL-STD-810G, SAE J1455)
ESD immunity ±15kV/±8kV — good for electrically noisy marine environment
Wide operating temp -40°F to 185°F
US manufacturer (Carlsbad, CA) — easy to contact
Configurable handle styles and output options
Inexpensive starting at $210
cons: 12-wire pinout (redundant sensors double the wires) — makes shaft routing harder
No CAN output — analog only (feeds into Teensy ADC or bridge circuit)
Hollow shaft unknown — the 12-wire count suggests wiring exits at base panel, not through shaft
Redundant sensors may be overkill for ThumbJoy application (adds complexity)
Glass/mineral filled nylon body — may be lighter duty than steel-bodied competitors
suitabilityReason: Excellent candidate. True IP67, 3-axis with Z-twist, 12-bit Hall effect, redundant sensors, stiff springs, US-made, $210. The 12-wire count from redundancy is a concern for shaft routing but manageable with the ATtiny bridge circuit. First call to make alongside JR Merritt CS3.

name: Ruffy Controls SG Series
category: 2-Axis Hand Grip with Optional 3rd Axis Thumbwheel
statusNote: Sample purchased 2026-03-30 for hands-on evaluation. KEY FEATURE: Has thumbwheel on top (optional 3rd axis in handle) — matches ThumbJoy throttle requirement natively.
axes: 2-axis standard (X/Y), optional 3rd axis thumbwheel in handle top
sensorType: Hall Effect — REDUNDANT (dual sensor per axis, safety-critical)
hollowShaft: Unknown — needs physical inspection of sample
pros: THUMBWHEEL ON TOP — optional 3rd axis in handle, exactly what ThumbJoy needs for throttle
TRUE IP67 sealed above panel — standard, not optional
REDUNDANT Hall sensors — dual per axis, safety-critical rated
Hand grip form factor — palm-sized, ergonomic
Optional operator presence switch (dead man's switch)
Variety of handle options — configurable
US manufacturer (Carlsbad, CA) — same as HE3, easy to contact
5VDC compatible with existing ThumbJoy architecture
Panel mount with standard cutout
cons: 2-axis base — no Z-twist rotation (unlike HE3). 3rd axis is thumbwheel only.
Larger form factor than HE3 (7.18" tall hand grip vs fingertip)
Redundant sensors = more wires to route
No CAN output — analog only
$271 starting price — pricier than HE3 ($210)
12-week lead time for production orders
suitabilityReason: Very promising for ThumbJoy. The built-in thumbwheel on top is the killer feature — eliminates the need to retrofit a Melexis sensor. IP67 + redundant sensors + hand grip form factor. Key questions for sample eval: 1) Does the thumbwheel feel right for throttle? 2) Can we lock X-axis with Y-gate? 3) Internal wire routing possibility?

name: Volvo Penta EVC2 Joystick (IPS/AQ)
statusNote: Industry-leading marine joystick — fly-by-wire docking for IPS & sterndrive. Part 24011, serial 2316335. Found at Kai Concepts shop.

videos.title: Volvo Penta Joystick Controls for IPS Docking (Official)
BoatTEST: Volvo Penta Sterndrive Joystick Full Review

axes: 3-axis (surge/sway/yaw) — push forward/back, side-to-side, twist to rotate
operatingForce: Proportional, boat-grade — two power modes (Low/High RPM button)
twistTorque: Twist knob for yaw (boat spins on axis)
twistRotation: Twist top to rotate boat — spring-return to center
lifecycle: OEM marine grade — 10+ years typical service life
price: ~$2,000-4,000 (part of EVC system, not sold standalone easily)
pros: THE gold standard for marine joystick docking — invented it for recreational boats
3-axis with twist yaw — push any direction + rotate with one hand
Two power modes: Low RPM (precision docking) and High RPM (windy conditions)
Fly-by-wire with sophisticated EVC software — individually controls each drive
Fully intuitive — no manual needed (confirmed by BoatTEST captain)
Self-preservation: won't shift above 750 RPM, protects drives
Massive install base — thousands of boats worldwide
Station-keeping capability in current
Side push feature for docking sideways against structures
Integrated autopilot engagement after course changes
Made by Volvo — automotive/marine engineering quality
cons: Locked to Volvo Penta EVC ecosystem — proprietary CAN protocol
Requires twin engine/drive setup (IPS pods or DPS sterndrives)
NOT available as standalone joystick component — always part of drive system
Expensive as system — $2K-4K+ for the joystick portion alone
Cannot be repurposed for custom small-craft CAN integration without reverse engineering
Grey color only, fixed form factor — no grip customization
suitabilityReason: Cannot be used directly (proprietary EVC system). But THE most important reference product — Volvo Penta literally invented marine joystick docking. Study the 3-axis feel, the Low/High RPM modes, the twist yaw, and the fly-by-wire philosophy. The unit found at Kai Concepts shop (P/N 24011) can be physically examined for ergonomics and mechanical design.

name: Dockmate TWIST
statusNote: Complete marine solution — already solved waterproof joystick for boats
pros: Already solved the marine waterproof joystick problem
3-axis proportional in one unit
Wireless with low latency
Can be handheld OR cradle-mounted
Controls 1, 2+ engines + thrusters
Proven on thousands of boats worldwide
REV G+ wireless receiver for easy integration
cons: Full system — hard to source just the joystick
Expensive for what we need
Proprietary wireless protocol (not CAN J1939)
Not designed for custom grip modification
May not allow Melexis thumbwheel integration
suitabilityReason: Excellent reference product showing what 'done right' looks like in marine. Not practical to strip for parts, but their ergonomics and waterproofing approach is worth studying.

name: Seascape Subsea ROV Controller
statusNote: New 2025 product — solved waterproof 3-axis + thumbwheel combo

videos.title: Seascape ROV Controller — Blue Robotics Community Launch

ipRating: Fully waterproof (designed for wet-deck ROV ops)
pros: Fully waterproof housing
3-axis joystick + analog thumbwheel in one unit
15 pushbuttons + 2 rotary switches
New product (Sept 2025) — cutting edge design
Designed for demanding marine environments (ROV ops)
Blue Robotics community integration
cons: USB HID output (not CAN J1939)
Handheld form factor — not panel mount
ROV-focused, not PWC/foiler
New product — limited track record
May not be available as OEM joystick component
suitabilityReason: Very relevant reference for waterproof joystick + thumbwheel combo. Worth contacting Seascape about OEM component supply or design consultation.

name: Mercury Joystick Piloting
statusNote: Industry standard for marine joystick docking — huge install base

videos.title: Mercury Joystick Piloting — How It Works

pros: Gold standard for marine joystick control
Millions of hours of marine operation
Intuitive 3-axis (including twist for yaw)
Fully proportional speed control
Proven waterproof marine design
cons: Proprietary SmartCraft ecosystem — locked to Mercury
Extremely expensive as a system
Not available as standalone component
Overkill for a small craft application
suitabilityReason: Not practical to use, but the absolute gold standard for how marine joystick control should feel and perform. Study their ergonomics.

name: MEGATRON TRY120
statusNote: DEEP-DIVE COMPLETE (2026-04-03). CRITICAL FINDING: Spring forces IDENTICAL to APEM HF55 (1.3N breakout, 2.8N operating). The 9Nm/350N values are MAX APPLIED tolerance, NOT spring stiffness. CAN J1939 at 250kbps default (500kbps custom available). Custom increased force/torque available for higher quantities.
output: Analog (0-5V, 0.5-4.5V, 0.25-4.75V, +/-10V bipolar), CAN J1939 (250kbps default, 500kbps custom), CANopen (250kbps + LSS), USB HID
breakoutForce: 1.3 N breakout / 2.8 N operating (SAME AS HF55!) / max applied: 350 N
operatingForce: 1.3 N breakout / 2.8 N operating — identical to APEM HF55
twistTorque: 0.09 Nm breakout / 0.12 Nm operating (SAME AS HF55!) / max applied: 9.0 Nm
mechanicalAngle: 36 deg (+/-18 deg from center) X/Y — wider than HF55 32 deg
twistRotation: 60 deg (+/-30 deg from center) Z-axis — same as HF55
ipRating: IP67 (genuine — designed for marine cockpit exposure)
mounting: Drop-in panel mount, 35.56 x 48.26 mm cutout, <26mm behind-panel depth
pros: Explicitly designed for maritime bow thruster applications
Y-gate limiter available (single axis Y option 8, plus guided feel options)
IP67 — marine-grade, all handle versions
CAN J1939 output (250kbps default, 500kbps/1Mbps custom available)
CANopen + LSS support
Ultra-compact 26mm behind-panel depth
Stainless steel shaft, metal internal mechanism
Multiple handle options including cobra (E/F/G) with up to 3 pushbuttons
Redundant sensor option available
Custom increased force/torque available for higher quantities
German manufacturer, established OEM supplier
12-pin Hirose connector — same type as Ruffy HE3
cons: Spring force IDENTICAL to HF55 — 1.3N breakout, does NOT solve soft spring problem
Z-axis torque same as HF55 — 0.09/0.12 Nm, no improvement
No hollow shaft (stainless steel, solid — prevents internal Melexis wire routing)
CAN J1939 default is 250kbps (Kai needs 500kbps — custom option available)
European sourcing — may have longer lead times to US
Premium pricing likely (German industrial OEM)
suitabilityReason: CORRECTED: Spring force is IDENTICAL to HF55 (1.3N/2.8N, 0.09/0.12 Nm Z). Does NOT solve the soft spring problem. However: IP67 genuine, CAN J1939, Y-gate limiter, compact 26mm depth, stainless steel shaft, cobra handles. Custom increased force/torque available for higher quantities. No hollow shaft.
dimensions: Panel cutout: 35.56 x 48.26 mm (1.40 x 1.90 in), behind-panel depth <26mm, panel thickness 1.17-3.17mm
connector: Analog: Hirose DF11-12DP-2DS(24) 12-pin; CAN: JST B06B-PASK-1 6-pin with Deutsch DTM04-6P

contacts.productManager: Christoph Haude (Head of PM Joysticks and Sensors)

recommendedConfig: TRY120 3 5 1 D 8 7 1 = 3-axis, rubber boot, spring return, Handle D (2 buttons), Y-only limiter, CAN J1939, drop-in. Request custom 500kbps + increased spring force.

name: W. Gessmann V24 / V28
statusNote: Modular German industrial joystick. IP67, CANopen, 20M cycle life, PLd/SIL2 safety. Very configurable but likely oversized.
lifecycle: V24: 20 million cycles, V28: 5 million cycles
pros: Extremely long life — V24 up to 20 million cycles
IP67 protection
PLd / SIL2 functional safety certification
Highly modular — combine axes as needed
German quality, established manufacturer (70+ years)
Multiple handle and grip options
cons: Likely too large for ThumbJoy beer-can housing
Modular = complex configuration
European sourcing, premium pricing
CANopen standard (J1939 availability uncertain)
Hollow shaft unlikely in modular design
suitabilityReason: Excellent engineering reference but likely too large and complex for ThumbJoy's compact form factor. Worth contacting if a larger base housing is considered in future revisions.

name: OEM Controls JS9
statusNote: US manufacturer with dedicated marine division. Online configurator. Multi-axis, hall effect, marine-rated. Contact: 203-929-8431.
output: Proportional analog 0-10V (configurable ratiometric/absolute), digital outputs per axis
pros: US manufacturer with dedicated marine industry division
Online configurator tool available
Designed explicitly for saltwater/vibration/extreme marine environments
Multiple handle options (compact, ergonomic, heavy-duty)
Return-to-center, detent, adjustable friction options
PLd/SIL2 safety capability (JS27 model)
Phone: 203-929-8431, responsive US sales team
cons: Exact IP rating unclear — 'marine rated' but not confirmed IP67
No CAN J1939 listed on JS9 — analog output only (would need Teensy ADC)
Specs behind their configurator/contact wall
Z-axis twist availability unclear for JS9 model
suitabilityReason: Promising US manufacturer with explicit marine focus and online configurator. Need to contact them (203-929-8431) for detailed specs: IP rating, Z-axis twist, hollow shaft, CAN options. The JS27 model may be better suited (PLd/SIL2, more options).

name: Shipcontroller Marine Joystick
statusNote: Purpose-built marine wireless remote with IP67 3-axis hall effect joystick. Proven in production boats. Component joystick likely sourced from major OEM.
lifecycle: Very long — hall effect, minimal moving parts
price: N/A (part of full system, not sold separately)
pros: Proven marine IP67 3-axis joystick in production
Hall effect — long life, no wear
Designed specifically for boat propulsion control
Z-axis rotation for yaw — exactly our use case
cons: Not available as standalone component
Integrated into proprietary wireless remote system
The actual joystick component inside is likely sourced from another OEM (APEM, MEGATRON, or similar)
No standalone pricing or purchasing
suitabilityReason: Valuable as proof that IP67 3-axis hall effect marine joysticks exist in production. Worth contacting Shipcontroller to ask who manufactures the joystick component inside their remote — could lead us to a direct OEM source.

name: MEGATRON 847 Series
statusNote: Ultra-compact IP67 hall effect finger joystick. 10M cycle life. Single-axis paddle style — could work as a dedicated thumbwheel replacement.
pros: IP67 waterproof — genuine
Incredibly compact (13mm depth)
10 million cycle life — double the HF55
Redundant sensor option
Soft-touch ergonomics
Could replace Melexis thumbwheel entirely as a standalone throttle input
cons: Single-axis only — not a joystick replacement
No Z-axis twist or X/Y control
Would be an additional component, not a base replacement
suitabilityReason: Not a base joystick replacement, but could serve as a standalone IP67 throttle paddle mounted externally on the ThumbJoy body — eliminating the need for Melexis thumbwheel and all 6 wires through the shaft. Worth considering as an alternative architecture.

name: VETUS Easy Docking Joystick
statusNote: Marine OEM joystick panel for bow/stern thrusters. IP67, combines twin joystick functions into single knob. Used on thousands of boats worldwide.
axes: Multi-axis (combined twin joystick in single knob)
pros: Proven marine IP67 design — thousands of boats
Combines multiple control axes into single intuitive knob
Child-proof lock feature
12/24V compatible
Dutch marine engineering (VETUS is a major marine OEM)
cons: Proprietary system — locked to VETUS thrusters
Not available as standalone joystick component
Panel-integrated, not a drop-in joystick base
90mm build-in depth — larger than ideal
suitabilityReason: Reference for how major marine OEMs solve the waterproof joystick control problem. The 'combined twin joystick into single knob' UX concept is interesting for ThumbJoy v2 design thinking.

name: Makersan MO 390 + MO 394
statusNote: Updated 2026-04-08 with code-level confirmation: MO390 supports Single Axis NS/WE + detent/friction modes (usable Y-lock behavior), and MO394 grip matrix confirms multiple vertical thumb control configurations. Still no confirmed spring-return Z-twist on this platform.
axes: 2-axis full, 2-axis plus, or single-axis actuation (Z-twist not confirmed)
output: CAN J1939, CANopen, or analog (MO394 grip analog-only in full assembly note)
twistTorque: Not specified (likely no Z twist in current MO390/394 base offering)
mounting: Joystick base with configurable grip and connector options
pros: IP67 stated by manufacturer
CAN J1939 and CANopen support
Redundant Hall sensing
MO390 code table supports Single Axis NS/WE and detent/friction behavior
MO394 grip code table confirms vertical thumb control options
Strong grip configurability: up to 5 front buttons + rear controls
Wide supply range options (5V or 8-32V listed)
cons: No confirmed Z-axis twist spring-return (requirement #4)
No published breakout/operating force curves found
Unknown shaft geometry for internal Melexis wire routing
No confirmed compact palm-sized geometry yet
suitabilityReason: Improved confidence for requirements #2 and #3 after code-level verification (vertical thumb controls + functional Y-lock behavior). Still blocked by missing confirmed Z-axis spring-return (#4).

requirementsCheck.thumbwheel_or_melexis: PASS (vertical thumb control codes confirmed on MO394)
requirementsCheck.xy_with_y_gate: PASS (functional equivalent via Single Axis NS/WE mode; explicit "Y-gate" naming still absent)

name: APEM HJ Series
statusNote: Discovered in Monday sweep (2026-04-06) via APEM distributor documentation. Strong force and CAN options, but sealing is typically up to IP63 front panel (IP67 only for some ball-tip handle configurations), so it fails ThumbJoy's mandatory IP67 no-exceptions requirement.
axes: Multi-axis hand joystick (vehicle in-cab class); specific twist variants not confirmed in this source
output: CANbus J1939, CANopen, USB, analog options
ipRating: Up to IP63 front panel (source notes some ball-tip handle configs up to IP67)
pros: Much stiffer operating force than fingertip class options (14N)
CAN J1939/CANopen plus USB and analog options
Multiple handle options
Industrial durability (5M cycles)
cons: Fails mandatory IP67 requirement in standard configurations (IP63 front panel)
Twist/Z-axis and Y-gate details not confirmed in source used
Likely larger than palm-sized ThumbJoy target
suitabilityReason: Rejected for ThumbJoy baseline because mandatory IP67 waterproof requirement is not broadly met on the listed configurations.

name: RunnTech RT02 Series (CAN 2.0 variant)
statusNote: New RT02 CAN2.0 listing found in Monday sweep (published ~2 weeks before 2026-04-06). Good CAN integration and button-rich handle, but only IP64 in the listing, failing mandatory IP67 requirement.
axes: Dual-axis in published example; 1/2/3-axis family variants mentioned
sensorType: Hall Effect or potentiometer family options

Rich grip controls (4 buttons + rear trigger in featured config)
Available in rugged industrial form factors

No confirmed vertical thumbwheel on top
No confirmed Z-axis spring-return in published CAN2.0 config

name: Makersan MO393 Thumbwheel Joystick
statusNote: Added 2026-04-08. Dedicated thumb control module with multiple spring-return/rocker/toggle variants. Not a full joystick replacement, but a promising way to avoid routing extra thumbwheel wires through a main joystick shaft.
ipRating: Unconfirmed on MO393 page (platform family targets IP67 class)
pros: Can offload thumb throttle function from main joystick handle
Multiple cap/actuation styles (spring return, rocker, toggle)
Redundant/full-redundant analog options
Simple wiring options (loose cable or Molex Micro-Fit)
Useful fallback if through-shaft routing remains problematic
cons: Not a complete X/Y/Z joystick base
CAN output not confirmed on MO393 itself
IP rating for MO393 variant needs explicit confirmation
Requires separate integration effort in ThumbJoy housing
suitabilityReason: Good creative solution for requirement #2 and the wire-routing bottleneck, but cannot satisfy full joystick requirements alone.
